text.title: Azure Landing Zones and Governance
text.summary: Eight design areas, management group hierarchy, policy-driven governance, and IaC tooling recommendations.

section.title: Landing Zone Design Areas
section.p: Eight design areas must be addressed before the first workload deploys. Retrofitting governance onto running workloads is exponentially harder.

list.item: 1. Azure Billing and Entra Tenant — tenant structure, EA/MCA billing, cost management
2. Identity and Access Management — Entra ID, RBAC, PIM, Conditional Access
3. Management Groups and Subscriptions — resource hierarchy, subscription democratization
4. Network Topology and Connectivity — hub-spoke or vWAN, Private Link, DNS
5. Security — Microsoft Defender, security policies, threat protection
6. Management — monitoring, backup, patching, update management
7. Governance — Azure Policy, compliance, tagging standards
8. Platform Automation and DevOps — IaC, CI/CD for platform team

rule.statement: Limit management group hierarchy depth to 3-4 levels; assign Azure Policy at management group level, not subscription level.
rule.rationale: Deep hierarchies are hard to reason about and debug policy inheritance. Management group assignment means policies apply automatically to all current and future subscriptions in the group.

example.code: Tenant Root Group ├── Platform │ ├── Identity (Entra ID, certificate services) │ ├── Management (monitoring, logging, automation) │ └── Connectivity (hub VNet, firewalls, DNS) ├── Landing Zones │ ├── Corp (internal workloads, no internet ingress) │ └── Online (internet-facing apps) ├── Sandbox (dev/test, relaxed policy) └── Decommissioned (quarantine before deletion)

rule.statement: Use Azure Policy Initiatives (groups of policies) rather than individual policies for governance themes — easier to audit and assign.
rule.rationale: Individual policy assignment creates hundreds of separate assignments. An Initiative bundles related policies (e.g., all security hardening policies) into a single assignable unit with one compliance dashboard.

list.item: Prefer built-in policies before writing custom — Microsoft maintains them for new resource types
Enable remediation tasks for policies with deployIfNotExists effect
Use exemptions sparingly; always set an expiration date on exemptions
Deploy policies via CI/CD pipeline — policy-as-code is auditable and reversible

rule.statement: Use Azure Verified Modules (AVM) for new Azure deployments; the ESLZ Terraform module is deprecated as of August 2026 and should not be used for new projects.
rule.rationale: AVM modules are authored and maintained by Microsoft with security best practices, semantic versioning, and compatibility guarantees. ESLZ is end-of-life and will not receive security updates.

list.item: AVM + Bicep: preferred for Azure-only projects; native Azure toolchain, no state management
Terraform + AzureRM provider: use for multi-cloud IaC or teams with existing Terraform expertise
ALZ Accelerator: automated Landing Zone deployment via GitHub or Azure DevOps pipelines
Bicep vs ARM: Bicep is the abstraction layer over ARM; never write ARM JSON directly
